topic.title: WARP Variants and Cycle-Accounting for Reported CPU Speed

header.para: WARP Variants and Cycle-Accounting for Reported CPU Speed

body.para: WARP collapses idle and delay spins by fast-forwarding the simulation clock to a known target instead of executing every cycle. This topic catalogs the WARP variants, the counters each one touches, the single safety rule that separates the clean variant from the quarantined ones, and a cycle-accounting scheme that turns the warp deltas into an honest "real vs effective" performance number. It closes by relating that number to the SRM CPU-speed field so the badge and the perf gauge stay cleanly separated.
The three counters (do not conflate)

1. Simulation clock -- cpu.cycleCount. The fundamental time base; advances per retired instruction AND by every WARP jump. The interval timer fires against it (cycleCount & kCchipTimerMask). This is the counter WARP is allowed to move.

2. Architectural cycle counter (RPCC / HW_CC) -- the 2026-05-11 split. It is cpu.cycleCount + ccBias, where ccBias is the offset the guest last wrote via HW_MTPR HW_CC. RPCC and HW_MFPR HW_CC both return that expression (IntArith.cpp). It is derived from counter 1, not independent.

3. Guest tick counter -- DRAM word at 0x3c970, the SRM uptime-in-ticks, incremented by the interval-timer ISR. This is guest state and must be advanced only by the real ISR, never written out-of-band.
WARP variants

table.style: border-width:0px; border-spacing:0px; border-collapse:collapse; cell-border-width:1px; border-color:#000000; border-style:solid; head-row-background-color:#00ffff; alt-row-background-color:none;

td.para: Advances cycleCount to the NEXT tick edge: (c0 | kCchipTimerMask) + 1. The FIRE path then runs the real ISR (bumps 0x3c970). One tick per hit.
Data-driven: reads the firmware's own counter (R5 addr, R6 target, R0 cur); writes counter to target and advances cycleCount by delta * kTickInterval.
Data-driven: R19 elapsed, R07 duration; advances cycleCount by (duration - elapsed) AND writes 0x3c970 out-of-band.

body.para: The safety rule

WARP may advance only the simulation counter (cpu.cycleCount / the system clock). The guest tick counter 0x3c970 must be delivered by the real interval-timer ISR through the normal FIRE path, never written directly. IDLEWARP obeys this -- it moves the clock and lets fireIntervalTimer deliver the tick. The RSCC-deadline warp violates it (out-of-band 0x3c970 write) and is quarantined. The delay-loop warps are otherwise sound (they are data-driven -- they read the firmware's OWN loop bounds, so they adapt to any clock), so re-homing them onto the IDLEWARP model (advance cycleCount, let the ISR post the ticks) is the path to un-quarantining them.
Cycle-accounting: the one new counter
cpu.cycleCount conflates executed and warped cycles. Add a single accumulator that captures only the warp deltas; everything else derives from the invariant cycleCount = execCycles + warpCycles.
uint64_t s_warpCycles; // NEW: sum of every warp advance
// at each warp: cpu.cycleCount += delta; s_warpCycles += delta;
uint64_t execCycles = cpu.cycleCount - s_warpCycles; // real executed cycles
The math (T = kProfileAlphaClockHz / 1024 cycles per tick; W = host wall-clock seconds)

table.style: border-width:0px; border-spacing:0px; border-collapse:collapse; cell-border-width:1px; border-color:#000000; border-style:solid; head-row-background-color:#00ffff; alt-row-background-color:none;

para.text: cycleCount / execCycles = ticks_total / ticks_exec = MHz_eff / MHz_real

body.para: Why the guest cannot see the warp (and where the gauge must live)

Inside the guest, RPCC (cycleCount) and the tick counter (0x3c970) advance coherently through every warp. Any guest-side measurement -- cycles divided by ticks, times 1024 -- therefore recovers exactly kProfileAlphaClockHz, warp-invariant. The over-performance K exists only relative to the HOST clock, so it is observable only by correlating guest cycles against host wall-time W. That is precisely what warpCycles plus a host timestamp provide. The gauge is an EmulatR-side readout (PROFILE line), NOT a guest-visible register.
Relation to the CPU-speed badge

The SRM banner "%3d MHz" comes from krn$_timer_get_cycle_time()/1e6, which on the ISP path reads the HWRPB cycle-counter-frequency field at PA 0x2070 (the same self-built HWRPB whose SYSVAR is at 0x2058). That field is functional -- the firmware's delay/timeout loops key off it, and the WARP thresholds are tuned against the current value -- so it must NOT be repurposed as a raw performance gauge. Keep it frozen (or set it to a true model clock only as part of the warp refactor, since a realistic clock enlarges the delay loops and only the re-homed delay-loop warp collapses them for free). The build-relative performance number (MHz_real, MHz_eff, K) lives EmulatR-side, computed from warpCycles + wall-time, independent of the guest.
Implementation order

1. Add s_warpCycles and increment it at every warp site (IDLEWARP now; the delay-loop warps when re-homed). Emit MHz_real / MHz_eff / K on the PROFILE line. Zero guest impact; immediate build-relative perf gauge. 2. Re-home the delay-loop warps onto the clean sim-counter model (advance cycleCount, let the ISR post 0x3c970) to un-quarantine them. 3. Only then, if a realistic banner is wanted, set the model clock at PA 0x2070 -- now free, because the re-homed warp collapses the enlarged delay loops.
See Also. Model Badging: SRM Platform Identity from an IIC Discriminator Device; Diagnostics: GuestMemory Store-Watch and Retire-Trace Arm; HWRPB - DS20.
